exercises.title: Why split(',') breaks on CSV
exercises.scenario: Naive split on quoted CSV returns wrong field count.
hints: split splits on EVERY comma — including those in quotes
"Smith, John" splits into '"Smith' and ' John"' — now 4 fields instead of 3
This is why real CSV parsers track quote state

exercises.title: Jackson readValue for simple POJO
exercises.scenario: ObjectMapper.readValue converts JSON string to Java object.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to parse JSON to a Product record.

blanks.hint: Method to parse JSON → Java object

hints: mapper.readValue(jsonString, TargetClass.class)

exercises.title: Jackson writeValueAsString
exercises.scenario: Serialize a Java object to JSON string.
exercises.instructions:
Use Jackson to serialize a Person object to JSON. Print compact and pretty-printed forms.

Expected output (compact):
{"name":"Aisha","age":28,"city":"Hyderabad"}
(then pretty-printed with indentation)
hints: mapper.writeValueAsString(obj) — compact
mapper.writerWithDefaultPrettyPrinter().writeValueAsString(obj) — indented

exercises.title: parseCSVLine state machine
exercises.scenario: Implement a correct CSV line parser.
exercises.instructions:
Implement `parseCSVLine(String line)` that handles quoted fields with commas. Test with lines that split(,) would get wrong.

Expected output:
Fields: 3
Field 0: Smith, John
Field 1: Engineering
Field 2: 95000
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;
// parseCSVLine state machine — handle quoted fields

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Fields: 3
Field 0: Smith, John
Field 1: Engineering
Field 2: 95000

hints: Track boolean inQuotes — toggled on each lone '"'
Comma when inQuotes=false → field separator
"" inside quotes → single literal '"'

exercises.title: Write correct CSV with quoting
exercises.scenario: toCSVField escapes fields that need quoting.
exercises.instructions:
Implement `toCSVField(String value)` that returns the value quoted if it contains commas or double-quotes. Write 3 product rows to CSV.

Expected output (first line of file):
name,price,description
"Wireless Mouse, 2.4GHz",799,Compact design
"Dell 27"" Monitor",18999,"4K, HDMI"
USB Hub,499,7-port

test_cases.expected_stdout:
name,price,description
"Wireless Mouse, 2.4GHz",799,Compact design
"Dell 27"" Monitor",18999,"4K, HDMI"
USB Hub,499,7-port

hints: Wrap in quotes if contains comma, quote, or newline
Escape existing quotes: replace '"' with '""'
USB Hub has no special chars — no quoting needed

exercises.title: Jackson JsonNode tree navigation
exercises.scenario: Navigate JSON without a POJO class.
exercises.instructions:
Parse a JSON stock object using JsonNode. Extract fields by name.

Expected output:
Symbol: TCS
Price: 3890.0
Change: +4.1%
Has volume: false

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Symbol: TCS
Price: 3890.0
Change: +4.1%
Has volume: false

hints: mapper.readTree(json) → JsonNode
root.get('key').asText() / .asDouble() / .asInt()
root.has('key') checks if field exists

exercises.title: Read CSV file with quoted fields
exercises.scenario: Use parseCSVLine to correctly parse a real CSV with quotes.
exercises.instructions:
Read an employee CSV that has a 'bio' field with commas and quotes. Parse correctly and print names and bios.

Expected output:
Aisha: 'Java developer, 5 years experience'
Raj: 'Sales lead, "top performer" award'

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha: 'Java developer, 5 years experience'
Raj: 'Sales lead, "top performer" award'

hints: Raj's bio has escaped quotes: \"\"top performer\"\" → "top performer"
State machine handles both comma-in-quotes and escaped-quote

exercises.title: Build JSON from data using ObjectNode
exercises.scenario: Construct JSON programmatically without a POJO.
exercises.instructions:
Build a JSON report for 3 employees using ObjectMapper.createObjectNode(). Add an ArrayNode of skills per employee. Print the result.

Expected output:
[{"name":"Aisha","dept":"Engineering","skills":["Java","Python"]},{"name":"Raj",...}]
hints: mapper.createObjectNode() for each employee
mapper.createArrayNode() for skills list
node.set('skills', skillsArray)

exercises.title: Parse JSON array to Java List
exercises.scenario: readValue with TypeReference for typed collections.
exercises.instructions:
Parse a JSON array of stock objects to `List<Stock>`. Print each stock's details.

Expected output:
RELIANCE: ₹2450 (+1.2%)
TCS: ₹3890 (+4.1%)
INFY: ₹1560 (-3.8%)
hints: TypeReference<List<Stock>>{} preserves generic type info at runtime
OR: mapper.getTypeFactory().constructCollectionType(List.class, Stock.class)
Stock needs no-arg constructor and public fields (or getters/setters)

exercises.title: CSV to JSON transformation
exercises.scenario: Read CSV, transform each row to JSON, write output file.
exercises.instructions:
Read a product CSV (name, category, price). Use parseCSVLine for safety. Transform to JSON array. Write to file. Print row count.

Expected output:
Converted 4 products to JSON
JSON preview: [{"name":"Mouse","category":"Electronics","price":"799"},...
hints: Read header line first → array of column names
For each data row: create ObjectNode, put(headers[i], values[i])
Add to ArrayNode, then writeValueAsString

exercises.title: JsonNode path navigation
exercises.scenario: path() returns MissingNode (safe) when key absent.
hints: path() returns MissingNode (not null) when key absent — safe to chain
asText('default') returns default if node is missing/null
path('address') → MissingNode; further path('city') → still MissingNode

exercises.title: Order summary: CSV in, JSON out
exercises.instructions:
Read order CSV (orderId, product, qty, price). Aggregate: total revenue, count per product, top product. Write JSON summary file. Print key stats.

Expected output:
Orders processed: 6
Total revenue: ₹38586
Top product: Mouse (3 orders)
JSON written.
hints: qty × price = line revenue; sum across all lines for total
productCount.merge(product, qty, Long::sum) accumulates qty-based count
Max entry by value for top product

exercises.title: JSON to CSV transformation
exercises.scenario: Reverse pipeline: read JSON array, write CSV with correct quoting.
exercises.instructions:
Parse a JSON array of employee objects. Write a properly quoted CSV. Fields with commas must be quoted.

Expected CSV output:
name,department,salary,notes
Aisha,Engineering,95000,Team lead
Raj,Sales,72000,"Top performer, Q4"
Priya,Engineering,88000,"Works on \"auth\" module"
hints: readTree returns JsonNode — iterate as Iterable<JsonNode>
path('key').asText() for safe field access
q() function: wrap in quotes if contains comma or quote

exercises.title: Multi-file CSV merge with deduplication
exercises.scenario: Merge two CSVs, deduplicate by key, write combined output.
exercises.instructions:
Read two CSV files with product data (sku, name, price). Merge with deduplication by SKU (file2 takes precedence on conflict). Write merged CSV. Print stats.

Expected output:
File 1 rows: 3
File 2 rows: 3
Unique SKUs after merge: 5
Merged CSV written.

test_cases.expected_stdout:
File 1 rows: 3
File 2 rows: 3
Unique SKUs after merge: 5
Merged CSV written.

hints: LinkedHashMap<String, String[]> preserves insertion order
File2 processed second — overwrites file1 entries for same SKU
SKU-002 appears in both: file2's 'Keyboard Pro' wins

exercises.title: Complete ETL pipeline: CSV → validate → enrich → JSON report
exercises.scenario: Full Extract-Transform-Load pipeline with validation and enrichment.
exercises.instructions:
Build a complete ETL pipeline:
1. Read product CSV (sku, name, qty, price) — parse correctly with quotes
2. Validate: qty > 0, price > 0, name non-blank
3. Enrich: add 'totalValue' (qty×price), 'tier' (PREMIUM>5000, STANDARD>1000, BUDGET)
4. Write valid records to JSON file
5. Write error records to errors.csv
6. Print summary

Expected output:
Valid: 4 | Invalid: 2
Total inventory value: ₹[sum]
PREMIUM: N, STANDARD: M, BUDGET: P
Files written.
hints: Validate: name blank, qty <= 0, unparseable numbers
Enrich: totalValue = qty × price; tier() function
Valid → JSON array; invalid → errors.csv with reason column
